Scott D. Ochs, PhD · Professor of Pharmacology · University of New England COM

Drugs for Glaucoma & Dry Eye Disease

High-yield pharmacology. Toggle ⚡ RAT Mode for PLO-only summaries. Click any slide to zoom.

IOP Emergency Threshold> 40 mmHg
1st Line (Glaucoma)Prostaglandins
1st Line (DED)Artificial Tears
Classes Covered7 drug classes
Physiology Prostaglandins ⭐ β-Blockers CAIs α₂ Agonists Cholinergics Drug Summary Algorithms Dry Eye Disease DED Drugs
PLO 1 — Glaucoma Physiology

Aqueous humor production & outflow — how drugs exploit the physiology

Know this cold and you can deduce every drug mechanism without memorizing it.

  • ✓
    IOP > 21 mmHg = glaucoma in a vignette (think open vs. closed)
  • ✓
    β₁/β₂ → Gs → ↑ adenylate cyclase → ↑ AQ production (blocked by β-blockers)
  • ✓
    α₂ → Gi → ↓ adenylate cyclase → ↓ AQ production (exploited by α₂ agonists)
  • ✓
    M3 on ciliary muscle → contraction → opens trabecular meshwork → ↑ outflow (pilocarpine)
  • ✓
    Trabecular = 75–90% (pressure-dependent); Uveoscleral = 10–25% (pressure-independent)
  • ✓
    Anticholinergics → close trabecular meshwork → ↑ IOP — contraindicated in glaucoma
PLO 1

Pathophysiology

Eye Physiology & Glaucoma Basics

Glaucoma = progressive optic neuropathy often associated with elevated IOP → visual field loss. In any vignette, IOP > 21 mmHg = glaucoma. Then determine: open vs. closed angle.

Slide 5 — Open-angle (trabecular meshwork patent but functionally obstructed) vs. closed-angle (iris physically blocks drainage). IOP > 21 mmHg = ocular hypertension.

👁️

Open-Angle Glaucoma (>95% of cases)

Trabecular meshwork slowly becomes obstructed. Gradual painless vision loss. Chronic — managed with eye drops. IOP typically 22–35 mmHg.

🚨

Acute Angle-Closure (≤5%) — Emergency!

Iris physically blocks trabecular meshwork. Sudden severe eye pain, headache, blurry vision, halos, nausea/vomiting, mid-dilated pupil poorly reactive to light. IOP > 40 mmHg.

Aqueous Humor Production & Receptor Logic

Slide 6 — Ciliary epithelium: β₁/β₂ (Gs) stimulate; α₂ (Gi) inhibits AQ production. Ciliary muscle M3 contraction opens trabecular meshwork. Schlemm's canal receives trabecular drainage.

🔑 Receptor Logic — Know This for Every Drug
  • β₁ & β₂ on ciliary epithelium → Gs → ↑ cAMP → ↑ AQ production → β-blockers block this
  • α₂ on ciliary epithelium → Gi → ↓ cAMP → ↓ AQ production → α₂ agonists exploit this
  • M3 on ciliary muscle → contraction → opens trabecular pores → ↑ outflow → pilocarpine does this
  • Carbonic anhydrase II → produces HCO₃⁻ that drives Na⁺/Cl⁻/H₂O into AQ → CAIs block this
  • Anticholinergics (opposite of M3): ciliary muscle relaxes → trabecular meshwork closes → ↑ IOP — contraindicated!

Slide 7 — Pilocarpine (M agonist): miosis + ciliary contraction + ↑ trabecular outflow. Atropine (M antagonist): mydriasis + ↑ IOP. Autonomic receptor table for the eye shown.

Aqueous Humor Outflow

Slide 8 — AQ flow: ciliary body → posterior chamber → anterior chamber → trabecular meshwork → Schlemm's canal (75–90%) OR uveoscleral pathway (10–25%). Drug sites of action labeled.

💡 Why Uveoscleral Route Matters (Prostaglandin Explanation)

Trabecular outflow = 75–90% but is pressure-dependent. Uveoscleral = only 10–25% but is pressure-independent. Prostaglandins that increase uveoscleral drainage remain effective at all IOPs — this is why they're more effective than β-blockers despite acting on the smaller pathway.

⚠️ Drugs That Worsen Glaucoma
  • Anticholinergics (diphenhydramine, TCAs, antipsychotics, antispasmodics) → block M3 → close trabecular meshwork → ↑ IOP
  • Systemic corticosteroids → ↑ IOP by unclear mechanism; also ↑ cataract risk
  • Sympathomimetics / decongestants → pupil dilation → can precipitate acute angle-closure
PLO 2 — 1st Line Drug

Prostaglandin Analogs — First-line for open-angle glaucoma

Most tested glaucoma drug class. Know every bullet.

  • ✓
    1st line for open-angle glaucoma; monotherapy preferred; all end in "-prost"
  • ✓
    MOA: FP receptor agonist → ↑ uveoscleral outflow (trabecular increase = controversial = NOT the exam answer)
  • ✓
    ↓ IOP 25–30% — most effective class
  • ✓
    Once daily at night; takes 4–6 weeks to evaluate response
  • ✓
    Key AEs: iris darkening (irreversible!), ↑ eyelid pigmentation, hypertrichosis of eyelashes, conjunctival hyperemia
  • ✓
    Bimatoprost: only PA FDA-approved for hypotrichosis of eyelashes (Latisse)
  • ✓
    NOT used in acute angle-closure — takes too long (4–6 weeks)
PLO 2

1st Line — Open-Angle Glaucoma ⭐

Prostaglandin Analogs (PA)

Slide 14 — PA highlights: ↑ uveoscleral outflow, ↓ IOP 25–30% (best), once daily at night, 1st line preferred, fewest systemic SEs, 4–6 week evaluation window. Bimatoprost for hypotrichosis.

Agents (all end in "-prost")

Latanoprost

Xalatan, Xelpros · QHS

Most commonly used; preservative-free (Iyuzeh)

Bimatoprost ★

Lumigan, Latisse · QHS

More potent; more AEs (hyperemia). Only PA for hypotrichosis of eyelashes (Latisse = cosmetic). Also: intracameral implant (Durysta).

Travoprost

Travatan Z · QHS

Preservative-free available; intracameral implant (iDose TR)

Tafluprost

Zioptan · QHS

Available only as preservative-free

💡 MOA: FP Receptor Agonist → ↑ Uveoscleral Outflow

Bind FP (prostaglandin F₂α) receptors in ciliary muscle → remodel extracellular matrix → ↑ uveoscleral outflow. Exam answer = uveoscleral outflow. Trabecular increase is inconsistent/controversial — will not be the right answer.

Why They Are First Line

  • Most effective: ↓ IOP 25–30% (more than β-blockers)
  • Once daily (QHS) — best compliance of all classes
  • Fewest systemic side effects — minimal systemic absorption
  • Takes 4–6 weeks to evaluate — do not check at 1–2 weeks

Adverse Effects

Slide 15 — PA AEs: conjunctival hyperemia (red eye), iris darkening (blue → brown, irreversible), ↑ eyelid pigmentation, hypertrichosis (longer/thicker/more eyelashes).

Adverse EffectDetails & Exam Relevance
Iris darkening (brown pigmentation)Irreversible. Blue/green/hazel eyes → may turn brown. Counsel patients before starting. NOT a reason to stop on exam.
↑ Eyelid pigmentationPeriocular skin darkening
Hypertrichosis of eyelashesLonger, thicker, more lashes. Basis for bimatoprost's Latisse indication. Bimatoprost = only PA for hypotrichosis.
Conjunctival hyperemiaRedness; more common with bimatoprost — switch to another PA if bothersome
Local irritation, blurred visionCommon to all ophthalmic drops
🎯 Exam Pattern

Open-angle glaucoma, IOP 28, no prior treatment → start prostaglandin analog. Patient develops brown iris discoloration from latanoprost → expected AE, not a reason to stop. Prostaglandins NOT used in acute angle-closure (too slow, 4–6 wks).

PLO 2 — 2nd Line

Ophthalmic Beta-Blockers

Second-line add-on when PA partially works; timolol is in the acute angle-closure protocol.

  • ✓
    MOA: block β₂ on ciliary epithelium → ↓ adenylate cyclase → ↓ AQ production
  • ✓
    Betaxolol = β₁ selective → fewer respiratory SEs → use in asthma/COPD. Less effective (most eye receptors are β₂).
  • ✓
    Non-selective agents: timolol, levobunolol, carteolol — alphabet rule does NOT apply to eye drops
  • ✓
    Systemic absorption → bradycardia, fatigue, bronchoconstriction (non-selective)
  • ✓
    Tachyphylaxis with long-term use
PLO 2

2nd Line — Glaucoma

Ophthalmic Beta-Blockers

💡 MOA: ↓ Aqueous Humor Production via β Blockade

Block β₂ (primary, ~90%) and β₁ (minor, ~10%) receptors on ciliary epithelium → ↓ adenylate cyclase → ↓ cAMP → ↓ AQ secretion. Betaxolol (β₁ selective) is less effective because most ciliary receptors are β₂.

Timolol

Timoptic · Non-selective · BID

Most commonly used; prototype; part of acute angle-closure protocol

Betaxolol ★

Betoptic · β₁ Selective

β₁ selective → safer in asthma/COPD. Less effective overall. Still some effect via β₁ on the ~10% of ciliary β₁ receptors.

Levobunolol

Betagan · Non-selective

Does NOT follow A–M selective / N–Z non-selective naming rule (eye drops exception)

Carteolol

Ocupress · Non-selective

Does NOT follow alphabet naming rule either

⚠️ Systemic Adverse Effects (from Nasolacrimal Absorption)
  • Bradycardia (all) — avoid in heart block, severe bradycardia, decompensated HF
  • Bronchoconstriction (non-selective only) — contraindicated in asthma/COPD; use betaxolol instead
  • Fatigue, depression, cold extremities, sexual dysfunction
  • Tachyphylaxis — ↓ efficacy over time with chronic use
PLO 2 — Adjunct / Acute Angle-Closure

Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibitors (CAIs)

Critical distinction: ophthalmic for open-angle adjunct vs. systemic IV for acute angle-closure emergency.

  • ✓
    All end in "-zolamide"
  • ✓
    Ophthalmic: dorzolamide, brinzolamide → adjunct for open-angle; sulfonamide derivative
  • ✓
    Systemic: acetazolamide (IV preferred, or PO) → used for ACUTE ANGLE-CLOSURE EMERGENCY; 500 mg IV
  • ✓
    MOA: block CA II → ↓ HCO₃⁻ → ↓ electrolyte/water secretion → ↓ AQ production
  • ✓
    Systemic AEs: hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis + renal calcium stones
  • ✓
    Topiramate (anti-seizure) also causes renal calcium stones — has CA inhibitor activity
PLO 2

Adjunct + Acute Angle-Closure

Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibitors (CAIs)

💧

Ophthalmic — Open-Angle Adjunct

  • Dorzolamide (Trusopt) · Eye drop
  • Brinzolamide (Azopt) · Eye drop

Less effective than PAs or β-blockers. Add-ons when 1st/2nd line insufficient. Sulfonamide derivative — caution with sulfa allergy (though usually tolerated).

🚨

Systemic — Acute Angle-Closure Emergency

  • Acetazolamide 500 mg IV (preferred — faster onset)
  • Acetazolamide PO if no IV access
  • Methazolamide PO — slower onset; pre-surgical only

Slide 20 — Acetazolamide: ACUTE angle-closure, mountain sickness, pseudotumor cerebri, urine alkalinization. Key systemic AEs: hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis + renal calcium stones.

💡 MOA: Block Carbonic Anhydrase II → ↓ AQ Production

CA II in ciliary epithelium: CO₂ + H₂O → HCO₃⁻ + H⁺. HCO₃⁻ (↑ concentration in AQ vs. plasma) drives osmotic movement of Na⁺, Cl⁻, K⁺, and water into AQ. Block CA II → ↓ HCO₃⁻ → ↓ electrolyte secretion → ↓ AQ production → ↓ IOP.

Systemic AEMechanismExam Note
Hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis↓ renal HCO₃⁻ reabsorption → Cl⁻ compensates → ↑ plasma Cl⁻, ↓ HCO₃⁻Most important testable AE
Renal calcium stones↑ urinary Ca²⁺; alkaline urine promotes Ca²⁺ precipitationAlso seen with topiramate (also CA inhibitor activity)
Paresthesias, drowsinessCNS CA inhibitionCommon with systemic use
↑ plasma NH₃↓ renal acid excretionAvoid in severe hepatic disease
🩺 Topiramate Cross-Connection

Topiramate (anti-seizure / migraine prevention) has carbonic anhydrase inhibitor activity → same AEs: renal calcium stones and metabolic acidosis. If you see these AEs with any drug, ask whether it has CA inhibitor activity.

🎯 Exam Pattern

Patient: sudden severe eye pain, IOP 58 mmHg, mid-dilated pupil, nausea → acute angle-closure → IV acetazolamide 500 mg + 3 eye drops. Do NOT pick prostaglandins (too slow, 4–6 weeks).

PLO 2 — Adjunct

α₂ Adrenergic Agonists

Clonidine-like drugs. Tachyphylaxis limits long-term use. Significant local allergic reactions.

  • ✓
    MOA: α₂ agonist → Gi → ↓ cAMP → ↓ AQ production
  • ✓
    Apraclonidine: prophylaxis post-laser surgery IOP spike
  • ✓
    Brimonidine: chronic adjunct for open-angle; somnolence, hypotension; respiratory arrest in young children — avoid in pediatrics
  • ✓
    Tachyphylaxis limits long-term use
  • ✓
    Most local allergic reactions of any glaucoma eye drop
PLO 2

Adjunct — Glaucoma

α₂ Adrenergic Agonists

💡 MOA: α₂ → Gi → ↓ cAMP → ↓ AQ Production

α₂ agonists activate Gi protein → ↓ adenylate cyclase → ↓ cAMP → ↓ active ion secretion into AQ humor. Net effect = same as β-blockers but through different receptor/pathway.

Apraclonidine

Iopidine · Eye drop

Primarily for acute prophylaxis of IOP spike post-laser surgery. High rate of local allergic reactions limits chronic use. Also in acute angle-closure protocol.

Brimonidine

Alphagan P · Eye drop

Chronic adjunct for open-angle glaucoma. Systemic absorption → somnolence, hypotension (like clonidine). Respiratory arrest in neonates/young children — contraindicated.

⚠️ Key Adverse Effects
  • Systemic: somnolence, fatigue, hypotension, dry mouth (clonidine-like)
  • Most local allergic reactions of any glaucoma drop — limits use
  • Significant conjunctival hyperemia
  • Tachyphylaxis — limits long-term efficacy
  • Brimonidine: contraindicated in young children — CNS depression + respiratory arrest
PLO 2 — Acute Angle-Closure Protocol

Cholinergic Agonists & Rho Kinase Inhibitor

Pilocarpine is in the acute angle-closure 3-drop protocol. Know brow ache as hallmark AE.

  • ✓
    MOA: M3 agonist → ciliary muscle contraction → opens trabecular meshwork → ↑ outflow
  • ✓
    Pilocarpine is in the acute angle-closure protocol (3-drop regimen)
  • ✓
    Pilocarpine (Vuity) FDA-approved for presbyopia
  • ✓
    Hallmark AE: brow ache (ciliary spasm/periorbital pain); also miosis, blurred vision; dosed up to QID
  • ✓
    Netarsudil: Rho kinase inhibitor → ↑ trabecular outflow; expensive; 2nd/3rd line; AEs: hyperemia, eye pain, conjunctival hemorrhage
PLO 2

Muscarinic Agonists + ROCK Inhibitor

Cholinergics & Netarsudil

💡 Pilocarpine MOA: M3 → Ciliary Muscle Contraction → ↑ Trabecular Outflow

M3 agonist on ciliary muscle → circular muscle contracts → pulls away from trabecular meshwork → pores open → ↑ AQ outflow. Same contraction also increases accommodation (useful in presbyopia).

Pilocarpine

Isopto Carpine / Vuity · Up to QID

Preferred cholinergic. In acute angle-closure 3-drop protocol. FDA-approved for presbyopia (Vuity). Can induce miosis for procedures.

Carbachol

Miostat · Eye drop

Less commonly used; same M3 mechanism

Netarsudil (Rhopressa)

ROCK Inhibitor · Once daily

MOA: Rho kinase (ROCK) inhibitor → ↑ trabecular outflow. ↓ IOP ~20–25%. Possibly inferior to latanoprost and timolol (2022 Cochrane). Very expensive. AEs: hyperemia, eye pain, conjunctival hemorrhage. 2nd/3rd line only.

⚠️ Cholinergic Adverse Effects
  • Brow ache — ciliary spasm → periorbital/forehead pain (hallmark, testable)
  • Miosis → blurred vision in dim light, difficulty driving at night
  • Transient myopia from accommodation changes
  • Dosed up to 4x daily — poor compliance vs. PAs (once daily)
PLO 2

High-Yield Reference

IOP-Lowering Agents — Complete Summary

Slide 27 — Summary table of all IOP-lowering drug classes, agents, and side effects.

Drug ClassAgentsMOAKey AEsUse
Prostaglandin Analogs ⭐Latanoprost, Bimatoprost, Travoprost, TafluprostFP receptor → ↑ uveoscleral outflowIris darkening, ↑ eyelid pigment, hypertrichosis, conjunctival hyperemia1st line (open-angle)
Beta-BlockersTimolol (non-sel), Betaxolol (β₁-sel), Levobunolol, CarteololBlock β₂ → ↓ adenylate cyclase → ↓ AQ productionBradycardia, bronchoconstriction (non-sel), fatigue, tachyphylaxis2nd line; add-on or substitute
CAI — OphthalmicDorzolamide, BrinzolamideBlock CA II → ↓ HCO₃⁻ → ↓ AQ productionRedness, stinging; sulfonamide cross-reactivityAdjunct (open-angle)
CAI — Systemic 🚨Acetazolamide IV/PO, Methazolamide POBlock CA II → ↓ AQ productionHyperchloremic metabolic acidosis, renal Ca²⁺ stones, paresthesiasAcute angle-closure (emergency)
α₂ AgonistsApraclonidine, BrimonidineGi → ↓ cAMP → ↓ AQ productionLocal allergy, somnolence, hypotension, tachyphylaxis; resp. arrest in childrenAdjunct; prophylaxis post-laser
Cholinergics (M3)Pilocarpine, CarbacholM3 agonist → ciliary contraction → ↑ trabecular outflowBrow ache, miosis, blurred vision; requires QID dosingAcute angle-closure; presbyopia
Rho Kinase InhibitorNetarsudil (Rhopressa)ROCK inhibitor → ↑ trabecular outflowHyperemia, eye pain, conjunctival hemorrhage2nd/3rd line; expensive

Slide 28 — Mechanism matrix: which class ↓ production vs ↑ trabecular vs ↑ uveoscleral drainage. Circled entries = confirmed mechanisms for exam. Yellow = unconfirmed (do NOT answer with these).

PLO 3 — Treatment Decision-Making

Treatment algorithms for open-angle and acute angle-closure glaucoma

These step-wise decisions are directly tested in vignette questions.

  • ✓
    Step 1 (open-angle): Prostaglandin analog monotherapy
  • ✓
    PA partial response (substantial but not at goal): ADD β-blocker (keep PA)
  • ✓
    PA minimal response: DISCONTINUE PA, switch to β-blocker (timolol)
  • ✓
    Acute angle-closure protocol: Timolol → Apraclonidine → Pilocarpine (1 min apart) + Acetazolamide 500 mg IV
  • ✓
    NO prostaglandins in acute angle-closure — takes 4–6 weeks
  • ✓
    IOP > 40 mmHg + declining vision = treat immediately
PLO 3

Clinical Decision-Making

Treatment Algorithms

Open-Angle Glaucoma Step Ladder

Slide 29 — Open-angle algorithm. PA 1st → substantial but incomplete response → ADD β-blocker → minimal response → DISCONTINUE PA, switch to timolol. Bimatoprost may work if latanoprost/travoprost fail.

  1. Start PA monotherapy (e.g., latanoprost QHS). Reassess after 4–6 weeks.
  2. Substantial decrease but not at goal? → ADD a β-blocker (timolol). Maintain PA. Can also add ophthalmic CAI or α₂ agonist as needed.
  3. Minimal/no response to PA? → DISCONTINUE PA, switch to timolol. Don't keep drugs that aren't working.
  4. Still not at goal? → Add ophthalmic CAI or α₂ agonist. Fixed-dose combination products available once regimen is established.

Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma (IOP Emergency)

Slide 30 — Acute angle-closure emergency protocol. IOP 40–70 mmHg typical. Sequential eye drops (1 min apart): Timolol 0.5% → Apraclonidine 1% → Pilocarpine 2% → Acetazolamide 500 mg IV. Treat if IOP >40 with vision decline.

🚨 Acute Angle-Closure Protocol — Give All Simultaneously

Clinical clues: Sudden severe eye pain, headache, blurry vision, halos around lights, nausea/vomiting, mid-dilated pupil reacting poorly to light, IOP > 40 mmHg (often 50–70), corneal cloudiness.

  1. Timolol 0.5% — 1 drop (wait 1 min)
  2. Apraclonidine 1% — 1 drop (wait 1 min)
  3. Pilocarpine 2% — 1 drop (wait 1 min)
  4. Acetazolamide 500 mg IV (or PO if no IV available)

❌ NO PROSTAGLANDINS — takes 4–6 weeks. Not in this protocol.

Drugs That Worsen Glaucoma (Drug–Disease Interactions)

Drug/ClassMechanismResult
Anticholinergics (diphenhydramine, TCAs, antipsychotics, antispasmodics)Block M3 → ciliary muscle relaxes → trabecular meshwork closes↑ IOP — contraindicated
Systemic corticosteroidsUnknown; may affect trabecular meshwork structure↑ IOP + cataract risk (chronic)
Sympathomimetics / decongestantsPupil dilation → iris pushed forward → angle narrowsCan precipitate acute angle-closure
PLO 4 — Dry Eye Disease

Dry Eye Disease — Pathophysiology & Diagnosis

Key differentiator from glaucoma: burning, gritty sensation, foreign body feeling — normal IOP.

  • ✓
    Formerly keratoconjunctivitis sicca; now called DED
  • ✓
    Symptoms: dryness, burning, gritty/foreign body sensation, redness, light sensitivity — IOP normal
  • ✓
    Causes: Sjögren's syndrome, anticholinergic drugs, estrogens, environmental (screens, wind, dust)
  • ✓
    Schirmer's test: paper strip in lower lid; ≤5 mm wetting in 5 min = abnormal
  • ✓
    Hallmark: hyperosmolarity of tears + ocular surface inflammation
PLO 4

Dry Eye Disease

Dry Eye Disease (DED)

Formerly called keratoconjunctivitis sicca. Multifactorial disease causing ocular surface dysfunction via tear hyperosmolarity and inflammation.

🧪

Pathophysiology

  • Altered tear-film composition (lipid layer ↓ → ↑ evaporation)
  • Reduced aqueous tear production (lacrimal/Meibomian gland dysfunction)
  • Hyperosmolarity of tears → ocular surface inflammation → vicious cycle
⚠️

Causes

  • Sjögren's syndrome
  • Anticholinergic drugs → block parasympathetic tear secretion
  • Estrogens
  • Environmental: screens, wind, low humidity, dust, heat
  • Aging, contact lenses, poor lid closure
Diagnostic ToolDetails
SymptomsBurning, gritty/foreign body sensation, redness, light sensitivity, blurred vision, paradoxical excessive tearing
Schirmer's TestPaper strip in lower lid; ≤5 mm wetting in 5 min = reduced tear production (abnormal)
Conjunctival injectionRedness on slit-lamp; bloodshot appearance
Tear osmolarity↑ osmolarity confirms DED; correlates with severity
🩺 Vignette Differentiator: DED vs. Glaucoma
  • DED: burning, gritty/foreign body sensation — IOP normal
  • Open-angle glaucoma: often asymptomatic until advanced; IOP > 21 mmHg
  • Acute angle-closure: sudden severe eye pain + headache; IOP > 40 mmHg; mid-dilated fixed pupil
PLO 4 — DED Treatment

Dry Eye Disease — Drug Step Ladder

Know the order. Identify each drug by name in a lineup.

  • ✓
    1st line: Topical lubricants (artificial tears) — carboxymethylcellulose, HPMC, polyvinyl alcohol
  • ✓
    Gels/ointments for severe DED or nighttime use (blurred vision — don't use during day)
  • ✓
    Cyclosporine (Restasis): calcineurin inhibitor → ↓ T-cell activation; 2nd line; ~$98/month; burns on instillation
  • ✓
    Lifitegrast (Xiidra): LFA-1 antagonist; 2nd line; ~$750/month; unique AE = dysgeusia (abnormal/bad taste)
  • ✓
    Varenicline nasal spray (Tyrvaya): nicotinic α3β4 partial agonist → stimulates trigeminal parasympathetics → ↑ natural tear production (nasal spray, NOT eye drop)
  • ✓
    Perfluorohexyloctane (Miebo): semi-fluorinated alkane → ↓ tear evaporation; ~$1,300/bottle
  • ✓
    Corticosteroids NOT recommended for chronic DED → ↑ IOP, cataracts. Loteprednol: only steroid with FDA approval for DED, short-term only.
PLO 4

DED Pharmacotherapy

Dry Eye Disease — Treatment

Step 1 — First Line: Topical Lubricants (Artificial Tears)

Slide 37 — Topical lubricants: drops (carboxymethylcellulose = Refresh Tears/TheraTears, HPMC = Bion Tears, polyvinyl alcohol = Refresh Classic, povidone = Soothe), gels (Systane, GenTeal), ointments (Refresh PM, Systane Nighttime) for severe/nighttime use.

Carboxymethylcellulose (CMC)

Refresh Tears, TheraTears

Most common artificial tear agent; good viscosity and retention

Hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC)

Bion Tears (also: hypromellose)

Similar efficacy to CMC

Polyvinyl Alcohol

Refresh Classic

Lower viscosity; good for mild DED

Gels & Ointments

Systane Gel / Refresh PM · Bedtime

For severe DED; use at bedtime (causes significant blurred vision)

🩺 Artificial Tears — Clinical Pearls
  • First line for ALL DED — OTC, cheap (~$5), safe, well tolerated
  • Preservative-free for patients with preservative sensitivity
  • Wait 10–15 min before inserting contact lenses
  • Wait 10–15 min between different eye drops
  • Refrigerating drops may reduce burning/stinging on instillation

Step 2 — Second Line: Immunomodulators

🔬

Cyclosporine (Restasis)

  • MOA: Calcineurin inhibitor → ↓ T-cell activation → ↓ ocular surface inflammation
  • Modest efficacy; not dramatically better than artificial tears alone
  • Takes months for full effect
  • Cost: ~$98/month
  • AEs: burning on instillation, conjunctival hyperemia
  • Wait 15 min before inserting contact lenses
🔬

Lifitegrast (Xiidra)

  • MOA: LFA-1 (lymphocyte function-associated antigen-1) antagonist → blocks T-cell adhesion to ocular surface → ↓ inflammation
  • 2nd line after tears ± cyclosporine
  • Cost: ~$750/month
  • Unique AE: dysgeusia (abnormal/unpleasant taste), irritation, ↓ visual acuity
  • Wait 15 min before inserting contact lenses

Step 3 — Newer / Specialty Agents

Varenicline Nasal Spray (Tyrvaya)

NASAL SPRAY — not an eye drop

MOA: Partial agonist at nicotinic α3β4 receptors (ganglionic) → stimulates trigeminal parasympathetic pathway → ↑ natural tear production. Same drug as oral varenicline (smoking cessation) but different route/formulation with fewer systemic SEs. Good for patients who cannot tolerate eye drops.

Perfluorohexyloctane (Miebo)

Eye drop · Newest FDA-approved

MOA: Semi-fluorinated alkane → forms monolayer at air-liquid interface of tear film → reduces tear evaporation. First drug specifically targeting evaporative DED. Cost: ~$1,300/bottle. Use only after failing other treatments.

⚠️ Corticosteroids — NOT for Chronic DED

May give short-term symptomatic relief. Long-term use NOT recommended due to: ↑ IOP (dangerous in glaucoma patients), cataract formation, corneal/scleral perforation. Loteprednol (Eysuvis) = only steroid with FDA-approved DED indication, but short-term only.

DED Treatment Summary

  1. Artificial tears — all patients start here. Add gel at bedtime for severe cases.
  2. Non-pharmacological: warm compresses, eyelid massage, punctal occlusion, screen breaks, environmental changes
  3. Cyclosporine (Restasis) — immunomodulator; ~$98/month; takes months
  4. Lifitegrast (Xiidra) — LFA-1 antagonist; ~$750/month; watch for dysgeusia
  5. Varenicline nasal spray (Tyrvaya) — if eye drops not tolerated; stimulates natural tears
  6. Perfluorohexyloctane (Miebo) — evaporative DED; ~$1,300/bottle; last pharmacological option

Eye Drop Administration — High-Yield Tips

🩺 Correct Eye Drop Technique
  • Wash and dry hands before use; shake suspensions
  • Make a pocket by pulling down the lower lid — instill drop into pocket (not directly onto cornea)
  • Rest dropper hand on cheek for stability; look straight into the tip
  • Close eyelid for 1–3 minutes — do NOT just blink (blinking pumps the drop out)
  • Nasolacrimal occlusion: press inner corner of eye with index finger for 1–2 min → ↓ systemic absorption (important for β-blockers and α₂ agonists)
  • Wait 10–15 min between different eye drops
  • Wait 10–15 min before inserting contacts after any eye drop
  • Refrigerating drops may ↓ burning on instillation
🎯 Exam Pattern — DED

Patient: burning, gritty/foreign body sensation, redness, no elevated IOP, first presentation → artificial tears (topical lubricants). Failed tears → cyclosporine. Cyclosporine failed → lifitegrast. Lifitegrast's unique AE: dysgeusia. Varenicline = nasal spray, NOT eye drop.

PLOs Only Active
